suspects.name: HIRKO JOSEPH
suspects.rationale: Top financial outlier with corroborating evidence tying him to off-book structures.

suspects.name: LAY KENNETH L
suspects.rationale: $80M change-of-control severance provision structured around the Dynegy window; outsized non-performance payout corroborated by third-party coverage.

suspects.name: KOPPER MICHAEL J
suspects.rationale: Surfaced via the communication graph as a key LJM-partnership actor (Fastow lieutenant); third-party corroboration.

suspects.name: SKILLING JEFFREY K
suspects.rationale: Documentary evidence across email threads of structuring off-book entities (incl. FCM) to avoid regulatory oversight; high non-performance compensation.

suspects.name: KAMINSKI WINCENTY J
suspects.rationale: Frequently discusses SPV/Raptor structures, but as risk officer WARNING against them — likely whistleblower, not perpetrator (unresolved).

suspects.name: LAVORATO JOHN J
suspects.rationale: Large retention compensation and trading role; flagged on association, weak concealment evidence.

suspects.name: FREVERT MARK A
suspects.rationale: Mark A. Frevert meets the Quiet-Money Rule with a $2M loan advance and near-zero email activity. His high financial outlier z-score (4.4) and central role in risk coordination place him at the heart of the risk reporting system. The June 13, 2000 email from Brent A. Price explicitly cites 'at the direction of Commercial' as a reason for non-officialized risk books, a unit known for off-book structuring. Frevert’s absence from any follow-up actions or communication on this issue, despite being a key actor in the process, implies complicity in concealment. No one else in Risk Management is mentioned in connection with this documented failure, and Frevert’s lack of email trail suggests deliberate obfuscation. This supports a suspicion score of 0.7.

suspects.name: PICKERING MARK R
suspects.rationale: Mark R. Pickering has no direct evidence of fraud, nor any record of financial anomalies. His only connection is through indirect references in Wall Street Journal articles and a single 'LJM Statement' email where he was mentioned as a recipient. He has zero email activity in the critical period, but no financial outlier status or loan advances. His high communication volume spike in late 2001 (19 emails in October 2001) is consistent with heightened awareness during the Enron scandal, not concealment. The evidence does not show deception, self-dealing, or pressure to stay silent. His role as CTO of Enron Net Works is a legitimate executive position.
